coredata.dc:description: The detection and imaging of α-synuclein fibrils (α-syn-F) is vital for pathological research and drug development of Parkinson’s disease. Herein, a red emissive fluorescent carbon dot (CD) with solvatochromism, named as NB-12, is designed with Nile blue and L-tryptophan as the carbon source. The density functional theory calculations are performed to understand the effect of nitrogen content on fluorescent property of CDs. The turn-on fluorescence of NB-12 is observed upon the interaction with the α-syn-F but not towards its monomer. The detection limit of NB-12 towards α-syn-F can reach as low as 9.5 nM. This high sensitivity is due to the wavelength blueshift of NB-12 towards fibrils to reduce the self-background of the probe and enhance the signal-to-noise ratio. Moreover, the fluorescence wavelength shift and intensity variation of NB-12 can be employed to establish dual-element fluorescence array but by one probe to distinguish individual amyloid fibrils as well as the protein mixture with high accuracy. Additionally, NB-12 has succeeded in imaging α-syn-F in brain slices of A53T mice and NL-5901 C. elegans. The present study offers a feasible method to construct the fluorescent CDs with solvatochromism for multi-mode detection of α-syn-F in vitro, ex vitro and in vivo.

Δλ represented the wavelength shift of the CDs in the presence and absence of α-syn-F.
The molecular model, LUMO and HOMO orbitals and HOMO-LUMO gap of (A) NB-12 and (B) NB-1.

Characterization of NB-12. (A) TEM image of NB-12. Inset: particle size distribution and high-resolution TEM image of NB-12. (B) FTIR spectra of NB-12, NB and L-Trp. (C) Full-survey scanning XPS spectrum of NB-12. High-resolution NB-12 XPS spectra of (D) C 1 s, (E) N 1 s, and (F) O 1 s.

(A) The fluorescence responses of NB-12 (5 μg/mL) towards different amyloid fibrils and monomers (5 μM). (B) The fluorescence spectra of NB-12 (5 μg/mL) towards α-syn-F of different concentrations (0.05–20 μM). (C) The linear plot of the fluorescence intensity of NB-12 versus α-syn-F concentration (0.05–20 μM). The LDA canonical plots for discriminating (D) four individual fibrils, the binary mixture of (E) α-syn-F with Ins-F, and (F) α-syn-F with Aβ-F at different ratios.

(A) The fluorescence decay curves of NB-12 in the absence and presence of α-syn-F. The determination of dissociation constant (K d) between (B) NB-12, (C) ThT, and (D) CR with α-syn-F. (E) The fluorescence spectra of NB-12 in glycerol-water binary solvents simulating different viscosities and (F) different solvents representing different polarities.

head.ce:title: A solvatochromic carbon dot with turn-on red emissive fluorescence for multi-mode detection of amyloids

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection and imaging of α-synuclein fibrils (α-syn-F) is vital for pathological research and drug development of Parkinson’s disease. Herein, a red emissive fluorescent carbon dot (CD) with solvatochromism, named as NB-12, is designed with Nile blue and L-tryptophan as the carbon source. The density functional theory calculations are performed to understand the effect of nitrogen content on fluorescent property of CDs. The turn-on fluorescence of NB-12 is observed upon the interaction with the α-syn-F but not towards its monomer. The detection limit of NB-12 towards α-syn-F can reach as low as 9.5 nM. This high sensitivity is due to the wavelength blueshift of NB-12 towards fibrils to reduce the self-background of the probe and enhance the signal-to-noise ratio. Moreover, the fluorescence wavelength shift and intensity variation of NB-12 can be employed to establish dual-element fluorescence array but by one probe to distinguish individual amyloid fibrils as well as the protein mixture with high accuracy. Additionally, NB-12 has succeeded in imaging α-syn-F in brain slices of A53T mice and NL-5901 C. elegans. The present study offers a feasible method to construct the fluorescent CDs with solvatochromism for multi-mode detection of α-syn-F in vitro, ex vitro and in vivo.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Protein misfolding and aggregation are implicated in the onset and development of many neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington’s disease and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis [1,2]. It is known that folded proteins misfold to form soluble oligomers and insoluble aggregates, which was claimed as important pathological hallmarks of neurodegenerative diseases [3–7]. For example, the α-synuclein (α-syn) propagation promotes the disease progression, and abnormal α-syn aggregates can result in the deposition of α-syn fibrils (α-syn-F) in the brain region, which are regarded as the pathological marker of PD [8–10]. Therefore, the detection of α-syn-F is meaningful for comprehensive understanding the overall disease process and development of effective therapeutics.

Nowadays, the fluorescence technique has attracted heightened interest for detection purpose, due to their noninvasiveness, economic expenses, operational convenience and fast response [11,12]. Fluorescence probe is normally necessary to produce the fluorescence signals, enabling the detection of analytes without fluorescence themselves. Carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon-based fluorescence nanomaterials, are one kind of popular fluorescence probe, known for their excellent photostability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [13,14]. Recently, Sun et al. developed red fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots for scavenging and detecting Alzheimer’s β-amyloid plaques [15]. Wang et al. also presented a human serum albumin functionalized CDs to image β-amyloid fibrils (Aβ-F), inhibiting Aβ aggregation and eliminating free radicals [16]. Despite the success of fluorescent CDs for imaging amyloid fibrils, the detection sensitivity needed further improvement. Due to undefined complex structure of CDs [17,18], tailor-made design of CDs was considered as a bottleneck to promote the sensing behavior of CDs towards amyloid fibrils.

In our previous studies, we proposed a method for synthesizing fluorescent CDs from small-molecule dyes, such as Congo red and Nile red, for amyloids detection [19,20]. These CDs usually possessed improved biocompatibility and better anti-photobleaching ability, in addition to inherit the property of the dye with turn-on red-emitting fluorescence towards amyloids. However, these probes still suffered from a low signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio and an inability to distinguish different proteins, limiting their practical applications. To enhance the S/N ratio, we shifted our attention to the microenvironment-sensitive fluorophore, which could be utilized to assist the CDs’ response towards amyloids by the microenvironmental change, e.g. decreased local polarity and elevated local viscosity during the protein aggregation process [21,22]. Among them, solvatochromic dyes were particularly promising due to their ability to shift emission wavelengths and modulate fluorescence intensity upon interaction with analytes, thereby reducing background fluorescence and improving the S/N ratio [23,24]. Particularly, even if only one probe was involved, dual parameters, i.e. fluorescence emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity, varied with the different analytes; thus, it is possible to combine these dual parameters to establish the sensor array to distinguish the analytes, elevating the applicability of the probe [25,26].

In this study, we proposed a kind of novel solvatochromic CDs to realize superior S/N ratio towards amyloids. Nile blue (NB), a hydrophobic solvatochromic dye with a classical D-π-A structure, emits near-infrared fluorescence and interact with amyloid aggregates [27,28]. Unfortunately, it was limited in real applications for in vivo imaging because of its quenched fluorescence response to hydrophobic cavity of biomacromolecules, small Stokes shift and toxicity on ecosystem [28,29]. Building on our previuos work, the NB was herein adopted as the carbon source for preparing solvatochromic CDs with red-emitting fluorescence [19,20]. To promote the solvatochromic behaviors of CDs, L-tryptophan (L-Trp), which was previously demonstrated to effectively promote the fluorescence response of CD-based probe towards amyloids [30], was also employed to enhance the nitrogen (N) content in CDs. Thus, the co-precursor, i.e. NB and L-Trp, in binary solvent of acetonitrile (ACN) and water was used to prepare the solvatochromic CDs, named as NB-CDs. The obtained CDs exhibited a high S/N ratio towards amyloids, and had the ability to distinguish the proteins based on the sensor array in terms of dual responsive parameters (emission wavelength shift and fluorescence intensity change). It succeeded in imaging α-syn-F in vitro, ex vitro and in vivo. The study paved a new avenue to construct the solvatochromic CDs for amyloids discrimination and imaging ( Scheme 1).

The materials and instrument information have been detailed in the Supporting Information. The preparation of insulin fibrils (ins-F), lysozyme (Lys-F), Aβ-F and α-syn-F were acquired as reported by our previous work [19,20].

The CDs were prepared through a simple one-pot solvothermal method. Briefly, a certain amount of L-Trp was dissolved in deionized (DI) water by ultrasonication, and 4 mg NB was dissolved in ACN to form a purple solution with a total volume of 4 mL. These two solutions were then transferred into a 25 mL Teflon autoclave, and heated at the fixed temperature for the prescribed time. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the red liquid was collected and diluted with 8 mL absolute ethanol, and filtered with a Millipore filter (0.22 µm) to remove large particles. Afterwards, the filtrate was dialyzed against DI water/absolute ethanol (1/1, v/v) with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to remove the possible residual chemicals. Finally, the solution inside the dialysis bag was evaporated at 70 ℃ to obtain the solid CDs.

To acquire the optimal fluorescence response of NB-CDs towards α-syn-F, the amount of L-Trp, reaction temperature, reaction time and volume of ACN were optimized during synthesis. For clarity, the obtained products and their corresponding synthetic conditions are listed and short-named in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of fluorescence sensor array for proteins discrimination
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solutions of target proteins (100 μM), including ins-F, Lys-F, Aβ-F and α-syn-F, were prepared and stored at 4 °C. NB-CDs stock solution (1 μL) was incubated with the individual protein (5 μM) in PB solution, and then subjected to the fluorescence measurement at the excitation wavelength of 565 nm to record Δλ (the wavelength shift of NB-CDs before and after the interaction with targeted protein) and fluorescence response F/F0 (F or F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of NB-CDs in the presence or absence of target protein, respectively), which were the two elements to construct the fluorescence sensor array. For each protein sample, five replicate measurements were conducted. The raw data matrix was provided in Table (S4-S7), and the data were transformed into canonical scores to draw the two-dimensional plot by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) using IBM SPSS Statistics 25 (International Business Machines Corporation, USA).

The geometry optimizations and frequency analyses of the models were performed using density functional theory (DFT) by B3LYP/6–31 G method based on 6–31 G (d, p) basis set [31,32]. All the calculations were conducted by the Gaussian 09 package.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging α-syn-F in brain slices by NB-CDs ex vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The mice (10 weeks old, male) were purchased from Changzhou Cavens Experimental Animal Co., Ltd (Jiangsu Province). Animal experiments were approved by Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology ([2024] IACUC 4192). To prepare brain tissue sections, the mice were deeply anesthetized with isoflurane, and the mice heart was sequentially instilled with 4 % paraformaldehyde and PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4). Afterwards, fresh brain was obtained and quickly fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde for 24 h. The fixed brain was dehydrated in the 20 % and 30 % sucrose solutions for 2 d and frozen in −80 ℃ refrigerator. Prior to slice, the frozen section was rewarmed at −20 °C and embedded with O.C.T. Compound (optimum cutting temperature compound), and the embedded section was sliced with a freezing microtome (Thermo NX50) to be approximately 20 μm for each slice. Afterwards, the obtained frozen slices were treated with 5 μg/mL NB-CDs or 20 μg/mL ThS, respectively. Finally, the labelled sections were washed with PBS and observed with the fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging α-syn aggregates in transgenic elegans by NB-CDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The imaging capability of NB-CDs towards α-syn aggregates in vivo was tested on transgenic model of Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) strain NL5901. For comparison, the non-transgenic Caenorhabditis elegans N2 (wild type, WT) were adopted as the negative control. Firstly, C. elegans NL5901 or N2 nematode were seeded at 20 ℃ in E. coli OP50/NGM solid medium. Their L4 adult worms were collected and washed three times with M9 solution and centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 1 min, and then treated with 10 μg/mL NB-12 in M9 solution for 30 min. Finally, the treated worms were washed three times by the M9 solution and anesthetized with levamisole (2 mM dissolved in the M9 solution) on a 3 % agarose pad for fluorescence microscopic observation.

For the turn-on fluorescence probe, the auto-fluorescence of the probe itself may have a significant effect on the probing S/N ratio, and thus the sensitivity. To acquire a high S/N ratio, diminishing the auto-fluorescence of the probe could be effective. The solvatochromic probe, as its name suggests, could exert an obvious emission wavelength shift upon interacting with target analytes different from that of the probe itself. Thus, it could be a good choice to reduce the interference of autofluorescence. NB possesses classical D-π-A structure with an amine donor and a charged iminium acceptor, which produces a strong intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) for solvatochromism [27]. NB exhibited an obvious blue-shift emission wavelength upon the low microenvironmental polarity, but red-shifted upon the elevating polarity. Besides, owing to the hydrophobic planar phenoxazine moiety, NB could bind to the hydrophobic β-sheet structure of protein aggregates [29]. However, NB is not an efficient dye for imaging amyloids as its quenched fluorescence towards hydrophobic biomacromolecule, small Stokes shift and toxicity on ecosystem [28,29]. Based on these considerations, NB was adopted as a carbon source to construct the solvatochromic CDs for amyloids detection, as some functional groups or carbon backbone of carbon precursors could be reserved while some properties could be improved in the formation of CDs [33]. Besides, the solvatochromic probe may exhibit dual changes (i.e. wavelength shift and fluorescence intensity) upon binding to amyloids, which would be employed to establish a sensor array for distinguishing the proteins.

Additionally, increasing N content of CDs played multiple roles to regulate the fluorescence property, solvatochromic behaviors and density of charge-carrier [34–36]. In our previous work, L-Trp, an amino acid rich in nitrogen and with good biocompatibility [37], was used as a co-carbon source to enhance the sensing performance of the resulting CDs towards amyloid aggregates [30]. This approach enhanced the hydrophobic interaction and offered extra hydrogen bond interaction between the probe and fibrils, while simultaneously targeted the hydrophobic β-sheet structure of protein aggregates. Herein, L-Trp was deliberately utilized to increase the N content, to influence hydrophobic and hydrogen bond interaction between the CDs and analytes, thus contributing to the solvatochromic behaviors of NB-derived CDs [38,39]. In addition, the enhanced N content could influence the density of charge-carrier and induce charge delocalization, which may facilitate the ICT of NB and thus make the resulting CDs more sensitive to the microenvironmental change [36,40].

Apart from the carbon source, the reaction medium was also vital for the fluorescence property of CDs in terms of polarity and boiling point [41,42]. Herein, ACN, as an additional solvent to water, was adopted, to not only assist the dissolving of NB but also reduce the polarity and boiling point of the reaction medium, which would benefit to form plentiful fluorophores and regulate the fluorescence property of the resulting CDs [41,43]. Based on these considerations, NB and L-Trp were employed as the precursor in binary solvents of ACN and water to prepare the solvatochromic CDs.

To achieve the optimal fluorescence response of NB-CDs towards α-syn-F, the amount of L-Trp was firstly investigated. As listed in Table S1, when the doping amount of L-Trp was increased from 0 to 40.0 mg (NB-(1−4)), the sensing response of resulting CDs towards α-syn-F was sharply enhanced and then decreased with F/F0 values of 2.46, 6.27, 16.0 and 2.00 for NB-1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively. Additionally, the similar blue-shift trend of wavelength to the change of F/F0 values was observed in Fig. 1A, with Δλ of NB-1, 2, 3 and 4 as 3.4, 6.6, 31.9 and 8.2 nm, respectively. The best sensing sensitivity and largest Δλ of NB-3 may be attributed to the involvement of an appropriate amount of L-Trp (20 mg) during the CD formation, which improved the interaction between the CDs and amyloid aggregates. EA was then conducted to characterize the N content within NB-(1−4) (Table S2). The N content for NB-1 in the absence of L-Trp was measured as 8.7 %, while elevated as 10.5 %, 10.7 % and 11.1 % for NB-2, 3 and 4, respectively. These data suggest that the N of L-Trp was successfully doped into the CDs.

For the purpose of understanding the effect of N content on the fluorescence property of NB-CDs, the orbital energy levels involved in NB-CDs were investigated by the DFT calculation. The possible molecular structures of NB-CDs were conducted by Gaussian 09 software. Based on the results of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Section 3.2), NB-12 may possess the amide bond formed by the carboxyl group of L-Trp and the amino group of the NB, and also reserve the functional groups from carbon sources, such as carbonyl and pyrrolic groups of L-Trp. Moreover, considering that the amount of L-Trp was much greater than that of NB in the preparation of NB-12, it was assumed that the sp2 hybridization inherent in carbon nuclei of NB-12 might be composed of one NB with one or more L-Trp molecule. According to the N content in Table S2, one possible molecular structure of NB-CDs with L-Trp (NB-12) was roughly deduced ( Fig. 2A). For comparison, one possible molecular structure of NB-1 without L-Trp was constructed in the same way (Fig. 2B). Consequently, the configuration of NB-12 and NB-1 was used for the DFT calculation. Their highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) were configured in Fig. 2A and 2B. The bandgaps of the two molecular structures were calculated to be 2.24 and 2.31 eV, respectively, indicating that the incorporation of N into CDs could lessen the gap between HOMO and LUMO. The result may explain that introducing N-containing structure into CDs could enlarge the conjugated sp2-domain, and thus promote the red-shift emission of CDs, which was in an excellent agreement with the emission of NB-1 (636 nm) and NB-12 (652 nm) in water. Hence, the improved N content may exert an effect on density of charge-carrier of the conjugated sp2-domain, and induce charge delocalization [36], which was contributed to the ICT of NB dye and thus solvatochromic behaviors of CDs. Reasonably, the NB-CD interacting with the fibrils might show more obvious emission shift after introducing N into CDs, which was in accord with the wavelength shift of NB-1 (3.4 nm) and NB-12 (49.0 nm) (Fig. 1A and 1D), and thus beneficial to enhance the sensitivity.

Based on the above analysis, a possible formation process from the NB and L-Trp to red-emissive CDs is proposed in Fig. S1. Under solvothermal conditions, NB reacted with L-Trp to form amide products, followed by dehydration and carbonization to form NB-CDs (Fig. S1).

Furthermore, the effect of reaction time (NB-3, 5, 6, 7) and temperature (NB-3, 8, 9, 10) on the fluorescence sensing ability of NB-CDs towards α-syn-F was also explored. As can be seen in Fig. 1B and Table S1, although the Δλ and F/F0 of NB-(3, 5, 6, 7) varied a little, the slightly biggest Δλ and F/F0 values were obtained at 8.0 h (NB-3). In addition, the maximal Δλ and F/F0 values were both acquired at 160 ℃ (NB-9, Fig. 1C). Thus, 8 h and 160 ℃ were chosen as the optimal to prepare the CDs.

Finally, the effect of the volume of ACN was investigated. As listed in Table S1, when the NB and L-Trp reacted in pure water, the F/F0 values of resulting NB-13 was 27.6 with the wavelength blueshift of 37.0 nm. In contrast, when the volume of ACN was elevated from 1.00 to 2.00 mL with reduced polarity and boiling point of reaction medium, the sensing response of resulting CDs towards α-syn-F was sharply strengthened and then decreased with F/F0 values of 30.9, 15.4 and 14.1, in accord with the wavelength blueshift of NB-(9, 11, 12) at 34.8, 36.0, and 49.0 nm (Fig. 1D), respectively. Although ACN was claimed as a N-doping agent in some reports, it can only be graphitized under extreme conditions [44]. In our case, ACN was not regarded as a significant N source, as evidenced by the EA results. The N content in NB-(9, 11, 12 and 13) obtained with different ACN-to-water ratios was comparable, ranging from 10.1 % to 10.8 % (Table S2). Thus, the reason why the sensing response of NB-(9, 11, 12 and 13) was different might be the change of polarity and boiling point of reaction medium to form different fluorophores.

Based on the above experiments, the synthetic conditions of NB-CDs were optimized. 4 mg NB and 20 mg L-Trp were dissolved in 1 mL ACN and 3 mL DI water, and reacted at 160 ℃ for 8.0 h. Thus-obtained NB-12 was used for the following studies.

The particle size and morphology of the NB-12 were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). As shown in Fig. 3A, the NB-12 presented well-dispersed quasi spheres with an average particle size of 1.94 ± 0.04 nm. The typical crystalline structure of graphitic carbon was clearly seen in the high-resolution TEM image of Fig. 3A, with the lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm. The AFM image in Fig. S2 exhibited the consistent cross-sectional heights of the NB-12 with the TEM result. Furthermore, FT-IR and XPS were used to ascertain elemental composition and surface groups of NB-12. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the typical groups in NB and L-Trp, such as O-H/N-H (3405 cm−1), C-H (2945 cm−1), CO (1672 cm−1), CN (1625 cm−1), CC (1585 cm−1), C-C/C-N (1112 cm−1) and C-O (1018 cm−1), were found in NB-12, demonstrating that these surface groups from carbon sources were possibly reserved in NB-12. In addition, the full XPS spectrum disclosed signals of C 1 s (284.7 eV, 82.5 %), N 1 s (399.7 eV, 9.32 %) and O 1 s (531.9 eV, 7.95 %) (Fig. 3C). For the high-resolution C 1 s band (Fig. 3D), three peaks centering at C-C/CC (284.7 eV), C-N/C-O (285.9 eV) and CO/O-CO (288.6 eV) were featured. As shown in Fig. 3E, the high-resolution N 1 s band revealed the presence of pyridinic N (399.7 eV), pyrrolic N (400.3 eV) and graphitic N (401.2 eV). Moreover, the high-resolution O 1 s band contains two peaks, stemming from C-O (534.2 eV) and CO (532.4 eV), respectively (Fig. 3F). Based on the XPS results, it can be further deduced that the NB-12 largely possessed the functional groups from raw sources, e.g. amino groups of NB or L-Trp and carboxyl groups of L-Trp, which was consistent with the FT-IR results.

The optical properties of NB-12 were studied by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As depicted in Fig. S3a, two absorption peaks at 227 nm and 273 nm were observed, associated with π→π* transition of aromatic CC bond. The broad weak absorption band in the 510–600 nm region was also found with the maximum absorbance at 551 nm, corresponding to n→π* transition of CO/CN. NB-12 exhibited almost excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence emission behavior (Fig. S3b), with the maximum emission wavelength of 652 nm and the maxium excitation wavelength of 565 nm (Fig. S3a). Obviously, the NB-12 showed a larger Stokes shift of 87 nm than that (45 nm) of the NB (Fig. S3c), implying that NB-12 could be more possible to reduce the interference of excitation light. Moreover, the fluorescence of NB-12 was quite weak with the quantum yield of 0.8 ‰ in water with reference to Rhodamine B. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of NB-12 remained almost stable from pHs of 4.86–8.00 (Fig. S3d), and for several weeks (Fig. S4), indicating its good pH and long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of NB-12 to α-syn-F in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the NB and NB-12 towards different amyloid fibrils was investigated. As photographed in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of the NB was quenched in the presence of amyloids, with the weakened fluorescence in the order of Aβ-F, α-syn-F, Lys-F and Ins-F, while the emission wavelength of the NB remained almost unchanged. Thus, the NB was unsuitable for in vivo imaging due to its quenched fluorescence intensity towards amyloids. In contrast, the fluorescence of the NB-12 was turned on upon binding with fibrils, with strengthened fluorescence from Aβ-F, Lys-F, Ins-F to α-syn-F ( Fig. 4 A). This phenomenon indicated that appropriate carbonization of the NB by reasonably selecting precursors could regulate the sensing performance of the probe towards fibrils, including the response mode and extent. Moreover, the emission wavelength of NB-12 blue-shifted 21 nm (λex= 550 nm, λem = 631 nm), 26 nm (λex= 548 nm, λem = 626 nm), 34 nm (λex= 543 nm, λem = 618 nm) and 49 nm (λex= 536 nm, λem = 603 nm) for Aβ-F, Ins-F, Lys-F and α-syn-F (Fig. S6), respectively, in contrast with the NB-12 (λex = 565 nm, λem = 652 nm, Fig. S3a). The Δλ would be ascribed from binding NB-12 with the hydrophobic β-sheet of the fibrils. In addition, the different wavelength shift (Δλ) and fluorescence response (F/F0) of NB-12 towards different fibrils might be caused by slightly different structure of the amyloids, which may be used as potential parameters to distinguish different fibrils (as discussed in Section 3.4). Specially, NB-12 maintained no change towards amyloid monomers, while the S/N ratio of NB-12 towards α-syn-F reached as high as up to 30.9-fold (Fig. 4 A).

To acquire the stable sensitivity of the probe towards fibrils, taking α-syn-F as the model analyte, the incubation time was optimized with the fluorescence response (F/F0) as the criterion. As shown in Fig. S7, the value (F/F0) reached plateau at about 30 min and remained relatively stable within 60 min. Thus, 30 min of incubation time was selected for the following tests. Additionally, the concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity change of NB-12 upon the interaction with α-syn-F of different concentrations was observed (Fig. 4B). An excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9930) between the fluorescence intensity (λex= 536 nm, λem = 603 nm) and the concentration of α-syn-F (0.050–20 μM) was obtained with an LOD of 9.5 nM (Fig. 4 C). Compared to reported probes, the NB-12 exhibited lower LOD or shorter detection time for α-syn-F (Table S3).

The fluorescence response of NB-12 to potential coexisting interferences was also explored, including metal ions (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+), amino acids (L-Trp, D-Trp, L-Phe, D-Phe, L-Met, L-Arg and L-His) and co-existing proteins (IgG, rnase, β-actoglobulin, glucose oxidase and cytochrome C). As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of NB-12 was only significantly turned on towards α-syn-F, while the studied potential coexisting interferences caused negligible effect, demonstrating good selectivity of NB-12 towards α-syn-F.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein discrimination by the sensor array based on the single probe
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in Section 3.3, NB-12 responded to different fibrils in terms of both varied Δλ and F/F0 values. Enlighten by our previous work that fluorescence sensor arrays could precisely distinguish analytes with similar properties in one single analysis in light of appropriate sensor elements [45,46], we attempted to use the NB-12 as the single probe to construct the fluorescence sensor array to distinguish amyloids.

The Δλ and F/F0 values of NB-12 were utilized as the two elements to construct the fluorescence sensor array (2 elements × 4 proteins × 5 replicates). The raw data of matrix was transformed into canonical factors in the two-dimensional plot to investigate the discrimination ability of this array based on the LDA analysis [47]. As shown in Fig. 4D, the four studied fibrils were separately clustered in individual groups with 100 % accuracy, suggesting that the established dual-element array exhibited excellent discrimination capability for these fibrils.

However, proteins may co-exist in some diseases, e.g. the mixture of Aβ-F and α-syn-F or α-syn-F and Ins-F in AD, PD and diabetes mellitus type II. It is challenging for the simple fluorescence sensor to distinguish the mixed proteins as most of fluorescence probes lacked the separation ability. From this respect, the fluorescence array is advantageous because it may discriminate mixtures of analytes with similar characteristics if the sensor elements owned sufficient cross-sensing ability. Herein, the potential of our established fluorescence sensor array to distinguish mixed proteins was evaluated, taking binary mixture of the Aβ-F and α-syn-F or α-syn-F and Ins-F with different ratios as the model mixed amyloids. As presented in Fig. 4E and 4F, the individual mixed protein was clearly clustered in each group without any overlap, and the classification accuracy arrived at 100 %. Also, the ternary mixture of Aβ-F, α-syn-F and Ins-F was clustered in individual groups with 100 % accuracy (Fig. S9). The results demonstrated the satisfactory discrimination ability of the established array.

Using NB-12 as the only single fluorescence probe, the dual-element array based on two independent responses (Δλ and F/F0) was established. It possessed good ability to distinguish individual fibrils as well as the protein mixtures, highlighting the unique advantage of NB-12 for detecting and discriminating amyloids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of fluorescence response of NB-12 towards amyloid fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the possible sensing mechanism of NB-12 towards amyloid fibrils, the fluorescence lifetime of NB-12 in the absence and presence of α-syn-F was measured. As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence decay curves of NB-12 with or without α-syn-F both confirmed single exponential fitting [48], and the lifetime was calculated as 1.54 ns and 2.28 ns, respectively. The result demonstrated that the tested fibrils significantly influenced the lifetime of excited state of NB-12, indicating the possible interactions between NB-12 and the protein aggregates [49]. To further explore the binding affinity of NB-12 with α-syn-F, their dissociation constant (K d) was determined, which was compared with ThT (a gold standard probe known for its turn-on blue-green fluorescence towards amyloid fibrils) and CR (a classical turn-on red-emitting fluorescence for fibrils). As depicted in Fig. 5B and 5C, the K d value of NB-12 and α-syn-F was measured as 0.3480 μg/mL, while the one of ThT and α-syn-F was 0.4521 μg/mL, and CR and α-syn-F was 4.957 μg/mL (Fig. 5D). From these data, we can see that NB-12 exhibited stronger binding affinity with α-syn-F than that of ThT or CR, which may be advantageous for imaging α-syn-F.

Considering that NB-12 largely reserved the functional groups of the NB (Section 3.2) and was expected to reserve some fluorescence property of it, we investigated the fluorescence response of the NB and NB-12 towards polarity and viscosity. The fluorescent emission of the NB (Fig. S10a) and NB-12 (Fig. 5E) were strengthened with the gradually elevated glycerol in glycerol-water binary solvents, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of NB-12 was viscosity-dependent and enhanced with the viscosity. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of the NB and NB-12 was calculated to be about 4.8-fold and 62.7-fold stronger in glycerol than that in water, respectively, implying that the appropriate carbonization may improve the fluorescence sensing ability of the probe towards viscosity. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of the NB and NB-12 towards polarity was tested in seven organic solvents (e.g. DCM, THF, 1, 4-dioxane, EtOH, MeOH, DMF and DMSO) and water. As shown in Fig. S10b, the λem of the NB exhibited an apparent blue-shift in non-polar solvents than that in water, and the similar behavior of NB-12 towards polarity was also observed. The fluorescence intensity of NB-12 was apparently stronger in non-polar (e.g. DCM, 1,4-dioxane) solvents. Additionally, NB-12 exerted more sensitive response towards polarity than NB. For example, 237.1-fold enhancement of fluorescence was obtained in 1,4-dioxane compared to that in water in the case of NB-12, but only 15.8-fold for NB. Meanwhile, as depicted in Fig. 5F, the λem of NB-12 in non-polar solvents also showed an obvious blueshift. As a typical evidence, compared to the most polar solvent (water), the λem of NB-12 in 1, 4-dioxane exhibited a 66 nm blueshift from 652 nm to 586 nm. These results indicated that NB-12 was more sensitive to polarity than the NB. The polarity of 1, 4-dioxane was reported to mimic the microenvironmental polarity of the β-sheet of amyloid fibrils [21], which could be an explanation for NB-12 to respond to amyloids.
Overall, NB-12 responded to the microenvironmental viscosity and polarity, and had an affinity with fibrils, which could be the underlying reasons for the response of NB-12 to the fibrils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photobleaching resistance and biocompatibility of NB-12
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the application, the photobleaching resistance of NB-12 was investigated. As shown in Fig. S11, the turn-on red fluorescence from NB-12 or CR after incubation with α-syn-F was observed. Obviously, subjecting to the continuous irradiation, the red fluorescence of the α-syn-F labelled with CR was sharply declined after 10 min, and almost faded at 30 min. However, for the α-syn-F stained by NB-12, the fluorescence signals almost maintained stable during 30 min irradiation. This observation indicated the good photobleaching resistance of NB-12 than CR.

Next, the cytotoxicity of NB-12 was evaluated in Human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells by the CCK-8 assay. As presented in Fig. S12a, NB-12 with the concentration of 0–100 μg/mL exhibited almost no cytotoxicity, while the NB exhibited serious cytotoxicity, i.e. the cell viability was below 10 % when the concentration of NB was higher than 40 μg/mL (Fig. S12b). Additionally, NB-12 had no obvious hemolysis effect (Fig. S12c). These results indicated that NB-12 was biocompatible and safe for in vivo imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging α-syn-F in brain slices with NB-12 ex vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Building on the superior fluorescence property of NB-12 towards α-syn-F in vitro, we further explored its imaging capability for brain tissues of A53T model mice (10-month-old, male) ex vitro. The A53T mouse, a transgenic model of PD with over-expressed α-syn-F deposits in the brain, was usually utilized for researches about PD related with α-syn-F [50,51]. ThS, a classical green fluorescence probe for amyloid fibrils [52], was used as a control. As photographed in Fig. 6, the fluorescence of ThS and NB-12 was faint in the brain tissue slices of wild type mouse, indicating that NB-12 had a low fluorescence background in the brain sections. As a contrast, the remarkable red fluorescence from NB-12 in the brain sections of the A53T mouse was noticed, which matched well with the green fluorescence from ThS (Fig. 6). The Pearson’s correlation coefficient between two was 0.67 (Fig. S13). This phenomenon illustrated that NB-12 can stain α-syn-F in brain sections of the A53T mouse ex vitro with low background interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging α-syn-F in NL5901 C. elegans by NB-12 in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the confident imaging results of NB-12 on α-syn-F in vitro and ex vitro, we further evaluated imaging capability of NB-12 in vivo. The NL5901 C. elegans, a typical transgenic model of PD expressing human α-syn aggregates tagged with yellow fluorescent protein (α-syn-YFP) in muscle cells [53,54], were adopted for this study. The C. elegans N2 (wild type) without expressing α-syn-F was utilized as the negative control. As shown in Fig. 7, the fluorescence signals on both green and red channels were barely seen in the head and body region of C. elegans N2, indicating that C. elegans N2 had no influence on the fluorescence of NB-12. In contrast, the red fluorescence was obvious for NL5901C. elegans stained by NB-12, and merged well with green fluorescence derived from the labelled α-syn-YFP (Fig. 7) with the Pearson’s correlation coefficient of 0.88 (Fig. S14). The result indicated that NB-12 had the superior imaging capability for α-syn aggregates in vivo.

In this study, a novel kind of solvatochromic carbon dot (CD), NB-12, was specially designed and prepared for multi-mode detection of α-syn aggregates. Nile blue (NB) was adopted as the carbon source, providing the response properties towards amyloids. Meanwhile, L-Trp was particularly employed to enhance the nitrogen content in the resulting CDs. Apart from its roles to improve the sensing performance and targeting ability of CDs towards amyloid aggregates, L-Trp could influence hydrophobic and hydrogen bond interactions between the CDs and proteins. Based on the DFT calculation, the gap between HOMO and LUMO was decreased with increasing N content, further illustrating enhancing N content could influence the solvatochromic behaviors of NB-derived CDs.

The fluorescence of NB-12 could be turned on by amyloids fibers. The NB-12 was used as the fluorescence probe for quantifying amyloids fibers in a concentration dependent way. In addition, the obvious wavelength blueshift was observed once the NB-12 interacted with amyloids, and differed with different amyloids. Hence, using Δλ and F/F0 as the two elements but only one probe, a fluorescence sensor array could be established for protein discrimination. The four individual studied fibrils, i.e. Ins-F, Lys-F, Aβ-F and α-syn-F, and their binary or ternary mixed ones could be well discriminated by the array with 100 % accuracy. Furthermore, owing to its good photo-bleaching resistance, biocompatibility and low background self-fluorescence, NB-12 succeeded in imaging α-syn-F brain slices of A53T transgenic mice and NL5901C. elegans.
This study provided a designable approach to construct the solvatochromic CDs-based fluorescence probe for detecting amyloid aggregates through multiple detection modes.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhang Jintao is now a PhD student in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong Uni-versity of Science and Technology, China. His research topic focuses on the novel fluorescent probes for diagnosis and treatment of neurodegenerative diseases.

Prof. Kun Huang is a full professor in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong Uni-versity of Science and Technology, China. He received his PhD degree from CaseWestern Reserve University in 2006. His research interests focus on mechanisticstudy and therapy of metabolic diseases.

Xu Yu received his Ph.D. degree from Wuhan University in 2013. He worked as a postdoctoral researcher at the Department of Biomedical Engineering, Pennsylvania State University, USA and the Department of Chemistry, Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Israel, respectively. Currently, he is an associate professor in the Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. His current research focused on microfluidic chip, biosensors, and nanotechnology.

Prof. Li Xu is a full professor in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. She received her PhD degree from National University of Singapore in 2009. Her research interests focus on separation and analysis of complex samples and application of functional materials in pharmaceutical, medical and environmental analysis.
